head.title: Haas Notebook 10.000A Haas Notebook 10.000B Haas Notebook 10.000C Haas Notebook 10.000D Haas Notebook 10.001 Haas Notebook 10.002 Haas Notebook 10.003 Haas Notebook 10.004 Haas Notebook 10.081 Haas Notebook 10.082 Haas Notebook 10.083 Haas Notebook 10.084 Haas Notebook 10.085 Haas Notebook 10.086 Haas Notebook 10.087 Haas Notebook 10.088 Haas Notebook 10.089 Haas Notebook 10.090 Haas Notebook 10.091 Haas Notebook 10.092 Haas Notebook 10.093 Haas Notebook 10.094 Haas Notebook 10.095 Haas Notebook 10.096 Haas Notebook 10.097 Haas Notebook 10.098 Haas Notebook 10.099 Haas Notebook 10.100 Haas Notebook 10.101 Haas Notebook 10.102 Haas Notebook 10.103 Haas Notebook 10.104 Haas Notebook 10.105 Haas Notebook 10.106 Haas Notebook 10.107 Haas Notebook 10.108 Haas Notebook 10.109 Haas Notebook 10.110 Haas Notebook 10.111 Haas Notebook 10.112 Haas Notebook 10.113 Haas Notebook 10.114 Haas Notebook 10.115 Haas Notebook 10.116 Haas Notebook 10.117 Haas Notebook 10.118 Haas Notebook 10.119 Haas Notebook 10.120 Haas Notebook 10.121 Haas Notebook 10.122 Haas Notebook 10.123 Haas Notebook 10.124 Haas Notebook 10.125 Haas Notebook 10.126 Haas Notebook 10.127 Haas Notebook 10.128 Haas Notebook 10.129 Haas Notebook 10.130 Haas Notebook 10.131 Haas Notebook 10.132 Haas Notebook 10.133 Haas Notebook 10.134 Haas Notebook 10.135 Haas Notebook 10.136 Haas Notebook 10.137 Haas Notebook 10.138 Haas Notebook 10.139 Haas Notebook 10.140 Haas Notebook 10.141 Haas Notebook 10.142 Haas Notebook 10.143 Haas Notebook 10.144 Haas Notebook 10.145 Haas Notebook 10.146 Haas Notebook 10.147 Haas Notebook 10.148 Haas Notebook 10.149 Haas Notebook 10.150 Haas Notebook 10.151 Haas Notebook 10.152 Haas Notebook 10.153 Haas Notebook 10.154 Haas Notebook 10.155 Haas Notebook 10.156 Haas Notebook 10.157 Haas Notebook 10.158 Haas Notebook 10.159 Haas Notebook 10.160 Haas Notebook 10.161 Haas Notebook 10.162 Haas Notebook 10.163 Haas Notebook 10.164 Haas Notebook 10.165 Haas Notebook 10.166 Haas Notebook 10.167 Haas Notebook 10.168 Haas Notebook 10.169 Haas Notebook 10.170 Haas Notebook 10.171 Haas Notebook 10.172 Haas Notebook 10.173 Haas Notebook 10.174 Haas Notebook 10.175 Haas Notebook 10.176 Haas Notebook 10.177 Haas Notebook 10.178 Haas Notebook 10.179 Haas Notebook 10.180 Haas Notebook 10.181 Haas Notebook 10.182 Haas Notebook 10.183 Haas Notebook 10.184 Haas Notebook 10.185 Haas Notebook 10.186 Haas Notebook 10.187 Haas Notebook 10.188 Haas Notebook 10.189 Haas Notebook 10.190 Haas Notebook 10.191 Haas Notebook 10.192

div.p: wihta´mʔanikʔahča = I will go along with you
ta´uc ti´mun(i) ha´-atac tic a´-an(i) = I go along the bank following the current
wik ta´ma anika´xtca = I will go with you
wik ta´ma anika´ha = I am not going with you
ta´ yun´kaxtc ti´tik maito´ro a´ra = A rope crosses the bayou (for a ferryboat)
a´m(i) ha´paxkin = I have finished going
kan´ hia´wic hi´xtc a´muxki = How did you (s.) go there; Read: he
a´nik ikya´ki = it is necessary for me to leave
ka´tǎn pro-form, ʔa´mʔɔn(a)hit(i) v.I la´wu(š) n
ka´tǎn pro-form, ʔa´mʔɔnawit(i) v.I la´wu(š) n
to´ni mi´ta pa´nʔuwaku = he who passes
ka´tan amo´nhit la´-u = Where did you (f. pl) go yesterday?
ka´tan amo´nawit la´-u = Where did you (m. pl) go yesterday?
to´ni mi´ta pan u-a´ku = the man who passes there
anantcian´ = I will be there (to do that)
ta´wăran i´taci ya´tara´pa = they go hunting to kill deer
ama-i´ ianan = Come seperately (when there are 2)
a´raha-inaxtca i´nima = we 2 are going to paint each other
asa´nisi´na = they 2 (f.) bet with each other
î´ma yantc ukă´-uxki = I (cause him to?) have kicked him
nixsa ăhahi´laxkini = I have begun to kick, I want to kick away
ikahaandaka´xtca săma ika´hahilonta = they will kick me
nixsatci´ic ăhahilakini = he is trying to kick him
a´haxtc = sometimes (G), no, its not that way (V.)
ta´-uc ăxsala = shallow water of river, sand bar
wic a´ka lu´pini = I drown myself
ta´xtcic aka´ta G ta´xtcik aka´ta V) = the sun sets
Henri ta-uc a´kawi tan wo´yu orsa´hai = Henry would have been drowned in the water had he not known how to swim
axkal ha´ixta tahenuk hanati lihonukiti = we get off the train
a´xkictik lă´wani i´lu = I pull my tongue back
ta ci´xkal a´xkita = beyond the stone
axkitate´xpuni = one third, "beyond (i.e. less than) one half"
ă´xsa a´xsa = to come, come again(?), Toward
ha´yina a´xsa = at earliest dawn, "clearness coming"
kari´ctik hăxsak = Come toward me! Come here!
rixk' o´xku a´xconi = a thicket of bushes (lit. young trees)
tiglek(i) = the end of the moon, "the moon finished", when its invisible
ta´xtcik unigu´xpan atci = sun halo, moon halo
kome´li ta´xtcik = ("Charnaie" the earliest grain (seed?) producing tree, 15-20 growing in bunches in the water) March
kome´li ta´xtcik te´hukumaktci = (sister of Mar.) April
(the informant did not know the others)
on haitcu talukin hekunic = An old person (woman) lived in a corner of the village
halukin aitcäni (ac´ɛ´ni) = it was a village
se´oxta hi´kîc axtca´ni = beads were in it
enacia´kawe rita´cle axtca = when he brought there was a fine house there
ta halu´kinic a´nita la´xspi i´kaxtci = I would go to town if I had money
tahalukinic a´talehe´ la´xspi tixkaxtcixtc = she would go to town if she had money
ik'haktci´ = Give me some of it!
ala (f = cane, arrow (because made of cane)
a´lată´tca wood thrush (Cr. oissan des cannes)
o´na´la a lazy man, a man good for nothing
alunta´pan harvest, "when you want to plant your crop" (tapa, vegetables, what is planted) (f.
ta´xsinta´lun brains "what is enclosed in the head"

tr.td: a lazy man, a man good for nothing
harvest, "when you want to plant your crop" (tapa, vegetables, what is planted)
brains "what is enclosed in the head"

div.p: e´cka ta´lun yi´kati = my marrow ('bone insides') hurts
nu´xtci nixsara tika´ma = a pretty girl
on uka´maxkatan i = I make fun of someone
sa´ku a markatan = I pretend to eat
kătc ama´rani J = I pretend to bite
a´mar i = to measure, a measure
ta´amar tcu´hilaki = I take the measure
ikha´li a´mari-la´xkini = I begin to measure my ground
uni ta-a´mar = to imitate a man
ta´xk a´mar = enough and no more, only
uko´wixkinda = they cause (a sick person) to sweat
ugru´pinda = they cause him to be purged
ahe´kinda = they cause it to be mixt
ta´-ucăna tixckira = the bottom of a lake (bottle, etc.)
ma´hon anani´ = I am seated doing nothing
ăp'ho-ugu´yani = I remove the cover and throw it away
a´parū m = cloud, clouds, heaven, sky, clear sky
taxce´laman a´rani = poor me! I am lying down
ha´pelo´rani = I am lying flat on my belly (f. hepi)
ă´rama = the common partridge (Cr. perdrix) hawk
a´ratu = almost (a doubtful form) (tu is perhaps the dim. suff.; Volsine gave almost as ho´tit-ak, but he evidently confused "almost" with "most all")
ă´sta mi´li = the red-shouldered chicken-hawk; (Cr. mangeur des poules)
ă´tca ădja = the common woodpecker (Cr. pique bois)
mi´hun(i) ătcara na´mu = many palmettos down there
ati´ra = just, just so far, just that much (probably related to tiric q.v.)
ixkinte´pinati´ra = my wrist "just the point of my hand"
hiati´ra-ta = just so far and no farther
o´ni ka´xcu sicioho´ hiati´ratak = as many mouths as men
hi´xtc hia´tiran = at the end of this time
atapă´ra = level, on level with, flat
ayehăhă mictîk = in a little while, before long
šuke´kuxta = I bend him (a m. obj.)
o´rwa o´ni maho´ni ate´'hpun = mixt blood
on o´cka ixpul hari´a = six inches, "half a man's foot"
e´xtu = quick (the quick movement one makes when scared)
kua´ta tǎ e´xtu = the red-headed woodpecker (?), Creole oisean peru
ri´xk' e´lu ri´pin = fruitful tree, acorn
ucpa e´lu = acorn or the white oak
e´luna = pokeweed, also given as a cactus that yields water; (Cr. chougras)
ha´xka uxku urku) e´pi = an ear of corn (G)
e´pu e´xpu = to fold, to bend, a bend
yu´nka ta eu´xtci e´pu = double sewing thread
e´ruk ta mi´ruk = my throat, neck
e´rūxsa ă´ruxsa e´ruxsa (V) ruxsa (J) = brambles, briars
wo´yu orsahāi = if he had not known how to swim
e´rusa igiahi´lgi = I begin to know
teckurahi´niku ǎronasa´ = the thunder is recognized as he
erusaxtcani = I know it is that
ē´yū = to put around, encircle, the neck (?)
e´yukani = I put around the neck, or over the shoulders
ta eyo´koi la´spita eyo´koi = silver buttons worn on costumes of Indians in later times
ta´yuta aka = silver pieces like little bells worn on the clothing
sǎ´yoxta sǎ-yo´xta sai-o´xta sǎ´yoxtaxtc = a necklace composed of silver pieces (V. and J.)
ha´ = what? (said when one has not understood a question)
ton he´ku kanaxkupon pan ya´wiksha u-u´xki = this man does nothing
ha-umuxtoniti mo´htu = one has wrapt him up
istar'k ha´-ic ha´-ixta) = mu upper lip
hayiti´ruk = scratched vertically (G), (lying sick in something) (V.)
ha´yihun = above (on the arm), above in a tree (V.)
e´xsin hamu´xkikan = I bend my head
yu´gmoxku harha´ria = the grass is tall in some places
ta´xkuk ha´ria ta´rik tigrikă´ha = this tree is not as high as the house
hai´xku ha´-ixku = other, another, unfamiliar, queer
ha´ixkulehi´ = queerly, very different, another kind
na´ra tahă´ha = the breath of a snake (given by Gatch- as "the hiss of a snake", probably erroneously)
ha-in hia´xkiti = one clears by cutting (the tall grass)
ha-in hia´kani = I clear or cut (grass)
ha´-ina po´kati = it is getting light
ha´yina a´xsa ha´yinaxsa = at earliest dawn
haka(ni) = to cut in a wedge shape as with an ax
h a´xkato´lu = dumplings ("round things made out of corn")
ha´xku o´rski ta´xspa = little sedge, "cypress with serrated leaves"
sa´gu ha´xparkatan = I pretend to eat
ha´xcupa lo´ta e´rusa = I can run fast
ha´xcupa tawă´han naxk = quick as lightning
ha´xtc ha´xtci = at present, now, just now
hax haxta = to open (as a door)
ticuk ha´xtan = I open the door, or passageway
Ta uc tǎ ha´-ixta ha´lukîn = Unkown to Sam; Point Coupie City, "place above the Mississippi" (i.e. on a bluff)
hal = to paint, make a figure or picture of
sa´ uk'ha´lan = painting of a dog
halaka´tan = I draw, make a portrait
ha´luhun ra-u´ = to put at the base of
ha´mi tă and ha´mitu were recorded by Gat.

as names of 2 birds the first of which he identified somewhat doubtfully with the spoonbill, the latter with the bustard; neither his own former informant nor Volsine recognized this and believed he had used hami for mali, g.
hapa ho´pa sagu = to finish, complete
wi´dj-ami hă´patixtc = when it (f.) has gone up and disappeared
sikya´na-le´pin'ha´pawi = he finishes speaking to them in a discourse
nu´xtci tahăra = a wise woman, "a watchful woman"
hu´mara tahă´ra = a keeper of a fast (the keeper of the cemeteries is called ta´waka, there is one under him called huma´ra tahă´ra; the latter has to report to him every day)
ta ha´ra hari´laxkini = I begin to sing a song
wic ko´ra ta ha´ra = a water drinker's song
(Words are in Choctaw and are : tanua´ hali´ huya´nî, naganî´ duwi´nga hali´, etc.;
ha´tcana kală´ran = I stand with legs spread apart
hă´ya = said of things shining; the sight, image
î´ric ik'hă´yakati = my nose is bleeding
he´ku = this male person or obj.
he´ktc = this female person or obj.
he´sin = these f. pers. or obj.
he´să´ma = these m. pers. or obj.
hă´tan ikroxpandasin = these things nearer me
ta ku´mac he´kina = far from the stone
kan heki´na = What is the distance?
he´kūx = to mix, to put to together
ta´ri ko´xpac ko´xpa) he´kunîc = corner of chamber
ta´xkin tca´ra he´kunîc = space between fingers (for he´kunîc also he´kun sin)
ta ni´xsara taxk ho´tu he´xpu o´rusa = this boy is able to climb all trees
hexsū kūra = to crawl, move (as snakes, clouds, etc.)
apa´rū he´xsuku´ra = the clouds move (V. & J.)
kidjuta tahe´muli = drawers, "inside of the trousers"
hē´ni = to salute, say good day
aha´ni nî´xki = one has said good day
uktaphe´nini = I salute him & shake hands
un ta he´ni = the salutation (of a person)
îkîk hepiniki´ uwa´na = my uncle wants me to remain here (G.) (Johnson could not understand this and thought it was incorrect)
ya´nic ci tăn he´riku = the bull bellows
un ukheruxkata´ni = I am steaming somebody
hēya = sister and fem. cousin of m., brother of f.
hi hin = that (something at a distance)
ka´n o´ni hin´ wi´gni u´xki = What person has told you that?
hin´hiatitc hîn tace´lama ikpo´kati = that is why I am finding myself so sad
hixtatik = on that side, in that direction
hic êra axkini = where I was reared, not long ago I was a young man
hî´ani = I wake you (f.) up (V. & J.)
ik'hi´ata = I want to play or have a good time (J.); (V. could not understand it)
hiapa = to carry on the back
ta´uc tak ta´ri hi´kira = name for a town in Louisiana, given by V. as "Mississippi"
recorded it as Baton Rouge but seems to have been in error
hi´xku to´lu = a mole with a pointed nose, shrew (?), "round (nosed) mouse"
hî´xcu = to plane, push; a plane
hiki´dju uk'hixcukani = I am pushing him into that (G.); I amm planing (V.)
hî´xcūka = (f. with ending -tc, or m. with -ku), dew
hil = suffixt aux., inch. "to begin to"
hindu´ wăran = Let us go to hunt!
hîndō´ = come on (V.), let us walk along
hī´na = to write, draw, mark, imprint
ro´hin(a) ta hi´na = crayon, pen; to print
wixci me´li ta hi´na = black ink
ri tacele´ tahi´na ta-ukin(i) = design to ornament a house, pretty marks put on
ro´hina ta hi´na ta po´lun = ink-well
rohimpo´ e´rusa = I know to read
ni´xkir hi´pu = dance accompanied by song (as in old times)
to´xkată hipu = The Blue-Headed Duck Dance
The Dances of the Tonikas and Biloxis, Choctaws
sa´-onan hi´pu = dance of a the wood-lice, both sexes
ka´pac hi´pu = dance of the chickens, 1 man and women together; 4 different songs
sa´tan hi´pu = dance of the horses; 2 sexes cross in files
to´xkată hi´pu = dance of the ducks; each line is one sex, and the lines approach each other
wahi-wahiē hi wahiē, ya´ v.I (and three others)
tcu´la hi´pu = dance of the fox; they dance behind the men;
Haliba´mu ka´pac hi´pu = dance of the chickens of the Alibamu
ructată hi´pu = dance of the sheep
wihi ho´ interj, wihiya´, wihihio; 3 songs before they turn
culi´k hi´pu = dance of the pumpkins (juromont, Creole word; Du Pratz giramont)
o´maxka hi´pu = dance of the alligator; 2 sexes hold each other's hand in a circle;
la´-u aha´ra hi´pu = dance of the daybreak; 2 sexes
tir´c tco´ha ta´wesa = dance of the commencement of midnight; a man leads the dance;
yehie´, hie´, hie´, yahie´, wahia´; they dance while flying about a tree.
Many dances were borrowed from the French.
They sang the calumet, laughing at the white people and the presents obtained.
The name of this ceremony was apanha´ra -- an old time dance he knows nothing about.
rixku ta´nin hî´rani = prickly ash, "tree which is medicine for the teeth"
o´ni lupi´ hi´ran = "dead man's remedy", a small low, red flower, 2-3 in. tall growing in wet places of the woods, etc.
yugmo´xku hi´ran tă ro´a = peppermint, "big white medicine herb" (Johnson said there are 2 kinds, but he did not know which was referred to.) Poss. sheepweed, l'arbre mouton
to´ni hita u´-aku = the man goes before you
to´niku hît uak kan otisan = What is the name of the man who is passing?
ho- hu- = outside of, out of
i´lu ho´-lăwakani = I pull my tongue out
ta´-ue mi´li hopălêkati = the Red River is overflowing
ho ya´na-antc = I will speak out freely
ăp´ho-ugu´yani = I remove the cover (taking it away entirely)
ho-ukloxsakan = I am drawing out of
ta ku´mac ho´waxta = outside of the stone
ta ho´xka = the hole, the boring
ri´xku ta ho´xka = mortise, "hole in the wood"
la´xspi tcu´run ho´xka = chain (stove-pipe, fire flue (G.))
ta noritu´xkuta ho´xkakani = I am boring a hole with a gimlet
ho´xsa nu´ma uxku = vines of the little grapes
ho´xsa nu´ma tă = vines of the big grapes
yi´ci ho´xsa nu´ma = the little grape
ho´xtci = to be lame, to limp
aho´mukan = I am dividing, I am partaking
timura ho´non = I descend the hill or slope
axkal ha´ixta tahenuk hanati li(u)honukiti = we get off of the train
tayiku honuxki naxk wiya´gati = do you they that the fire is extinct?
hot´ ho´togi = they are all finished, i.e. dead
ho´tu le´he = all up (i.e. all get up) (J.)
e´ckipura hu´akan = I wash my backsides
e´hukumawa´li = the son of my father's sister
hu´xpi = common robin, also given as the little blue heron; by Gat. as the dove, and by Johnson as the "snowbird"; the Cr. name is petit gris. Wren.
ta´lu hu´cu = ear-lobe for putting ring in
hu´ma mi´li = red berry, and thus "strawberry"
hūmara = fasting, chaste, abstaining (during fasts a man ate only one meal a day, and this not before noon, and he did not even so much as speak to a woman)
nuxtc humara´ = a chaste woman, virgin
hupa = to scratch (this is very doubtful)
ro´wa ta´ran irukati huri´wi = the sail (or flag) flaps in the wind
hutci = petticoat, robe; to put on a petticoat, robe
nuxtci hu´tci yu´ru = a long petticoat
nuxtci hu´tci yu´roho = a short petticoat
nuxtci hu´tci ta-i´ran nokc ta´ke = a bear skin robe
nuxtci hu´tci ta-i´ran ya´nic ka´xci = a buffalo skin robe
histikăhă uki´makan = I am pushing away or aside (from someone)
i´ra = to put on clothing, dress
yăm irakura = he is to wear fine clothing
o´ni i´ra pa´ha = a naked man
ti´ra ta´nokini e´puni = I fold this web; a loom
ti´ra ta´nokin(i) huta´yin(i) = I extend a web
G-S have y's word for spider ši´laka´lu n
ti´ra ko´tu = common blanket, or covering
ructa tă u´pul ti´ra ko´ta = woolen blanket
nuxtc hu´tci ta-i´ran ya´ ta´xkic = robe of deer skin
nuxtc hu´tci v.I ta-i´ran ructa n ta´xkic
nuxtc hu´tci v.I ta-i´ran yaci v.st ta´xkic
ro´wa ta´ra irukati huri´wi = the flag flaps in the wind
ta i´tca ta i´tca = (f.) the flesh
iu´wî = cicada (cr. la cigale), also given a katydid and locust
iyi´ = (m.) mash potato ( Sagittaria ; Choc. ahe´ )
often employed with interrogatives and impertatives (prob.
kac yu´kati yakî´k = When will you come?
kan hia´ti hîn wa´lik = why do you cry?
kak hin´hi-a-uk = who has done that?
ta´lun ta´la la´pu ki kan ho´tu ma´xkaha = if the harvest of the plantation is good things will be cheap
yakanika´xtcaki la´hon tuk ya´kanika´xtca = if I come, I will come in the morning
sicîrkatanîk igia´gi ha´xtci = I am obliged to aid them (G), he came and helped me (J)
ka = (indefinite pronoun), something, what, where, etc.
kat ka´ta ka´tan = where? to what place?
ka´ctik tǎ´naha = I walk in zigzags
ka´xtatik = From which bank of the river?
ka´xchu he´ku = How much does this (cost)?
kaxt ( kat ) ho´tu = all the directions, or sides
ka-ati´ra ta´-ue yukana'ra = to where the water reaches
ik so´lu la'pu yatăhăhă ka´han paha´ = If I am not cured there is no payment
sigio´yan ka´hati = their labors are paid for
i'kaha-anda´xtca = they are going to pay me
ti´tik a'kahani = confluence of 2 bayous or rivers
ka´hi tapita = a floor to walk on (V.) steps (J.)
pi´ta ta ka´xtini = steps of a ladder
ri´xku tcu´ri ta ka´ti = a step ladder
ri´xku tcu´ri ta kaxtini = a step-ladder
ko´xsuk uk ta ka´xpuni = nippers of crawfish
ka´xci ya´naka = I tell the truth
ka´cilehe = that's the truth, to sneeze (E.)
hin îk kăxtciti = you bite me
ikraxpundi´ra kaxtigani = I button my coat
tircuk ta kaxti´ = lock of the door
tixcuk to´ru ta ka´xti = latch, lock across door
ta ka´xti maho´ni = latch with cord ("for nothing" because it has no real key)
ka´li = to set something straight up, to stand erect
ăcka tirictan kaläran = I stand on my toes
äcka a´nupal kală´ran(i) = I stand with knees joined
ha´tcana kalǎ´ran = I stand with legs spread asunder
ama´yi wăhasak ta ci´xkal kalurana´ = the stones are standing on opp. banks
tu´il ukalixta´ni = I bend some things, it leans on one side
hokalinikaxtca = I will set it forth ( a speech or description )
kalu = to make a clucking noise, glu, glu
kalu´kani = sing glu, glu ( like a turkey )
kana = cross, 2) to walk, ie. to cross the legs, to step
ka´nan ra´-ukani = I cross and put it down
ka´nan ka´lini = the cross planted, erected (G). standing with one leg behind the other (V.)
ka´ra kă´ra = marbled, mottled; f. kara´ktci
ta ku´mac ka´rixta = on this side of the stone
tă´kar ya´kan = I bend to one side
ha-ic kă´ra po´kan = I look up
tă´kar a´rani = I am lying on one side
ko´xsuki ta ka´ru = the black-capped night-heron
eya ikăxckaki = I sprained my arm
igatcisa´hu = my stepmother (G.), my mother's sister (V.)
katawi´ya = I stop (J.), where are you going?
ri´xku ki´tcta ke´rini = to notch in wood (as ornaments)
o'ni yugmo´xku ta ke´ri = a mower
kixka ki´rka kicka = (m.) mollusk; the palate
u´xcik ta kixka = fresh water mussel, oyster
ucioho´ ta kixka = palate of the mouth
ki´xtita ikelogi = I have the habit of pinching
kō´-ō kō = haha! bravo! good! well! (exclamation of satisfaction)
kō´hina = a dish, pot, piece of earthenware, ceramic
ri ko´xpac ko´hin = a china pot
a´la ko´xku = terrapin, "turtle in the cave"
kɔ´satahk ʔikʔe´lʔɔki = I have the habit of scratching
ri ticte´nir ko´xpa = wall, partition wall
ta´xtcik unigu´xpan a´tci = a sun halo or moon halo
imayantc uko´xsonta = I cause to scratch him
ko´xsotaxk ike´logi = I have the habit of scratching

The first of these means " crawfish shaman "; the rainbow was supposed to be the painting on the face of a shaman in the sky who at time sucks up little fishes and crawfish and lets them fall back again upon the earth.
supposes that this very things was done by whirlwinds at times and the myth created to explain the phenomenon; ko´xsuk yo´wic is said to mean " crawfish drawing ")
komu ko´muta = pl. (with name of the thing)
ko´ra ko´ran ko´ran ko´raktc = (m.) round, a circle; (f.) round, a circle
e´xsin ko´ra = crown of the head; also scalp (?)
ko´rini = to assemble (refers to a custom of assembling to dance around the dead)
kuxn ku´rini kun ku´rini = drum, tambour
e´cku sa´dji ko´rini = stormy weather, "a multitude or an assembly of rainstorms"
wi´ci-kuru´ hä´ntcuk = a drop of water (V.) (?)
ko´ra ko´ran = to drink, a drink
wi´ci ko´rêiksi´hu = I am very thirsty
wi´ci ta ko´ra = light beverage (applied to soft drinks)
wi´ci to´raxtan ta ko´ra = an alcoholic drink
ci´la ta ko´ra = fern sp. (Indian medicine) (G.)
wărani ta ko´ra = a polypod which grows on the bark of green wood, "hunting medicine"
la´psi ko´tcu = a bell, "metal short" (V. & J)
la´psi ko´tcu tenin axki´ = I have rung a bell
ukko´tc oxkini = I have cut him shorter
tcu´xki kâ´ta = white oak or post oak
gives this as meaning " building " but this seems very doubtful.
It occurs in the following combinations :
ko´tiyu´ki ko´tiuki = hominy, Cr. gros grue
te´xkluka´yi ko´wakani = I am peeling an orange
ho´xsan ko´wini = lianas, big crooked vine
ho´xsan ko´winta = with rollers made of lianas
kua´ta ta e´xtu = red-headed woodpecker (J.), cowbird (G.)
ku´a ha´icta-po´ = "bird looking upward", sun-gazer, or Am. bittern; Cr. vise en l'air
ku´a mi´lită = the scarlet tanager; Cr. anispatule
The male is colored red but is without a crest.
There is a story regarding this bird and the cardinal.
wic lo´ki ku´a = "water pond bird", the tame duck (V. & J.); Gat. called this the "wagtail" prob. erroneously
ta´niniku wo´yuku uktakuinta = the fish swims with its fins
una´la kûxpa ho´tuna = they 2 finished putting feathers on their arrows
ructata´ ux kuntc(u) = sheep dung, defecate
ta ku´mac ri = a stone house
kuxn ku´rini kun ku´rin(i) = drum, tambour
kunaca ya´gi ku´naca ya´aki = it has gotten dark
ku´tca = to crush, tramp on something
kukudja´kani = I crush many times (as in the case of brains which it is desire to make penetrate skins thoroughly when tanning them)
e´xkutu sahok = my brother's son, my nephex, "my other son"
e´xkuto wa´lîk = my stepson (G.), cousin (son of father's brother) (J.)
tco´ha pia´xkinta = past (pf.) I have been chief (and am no longer)
la´tika a´ha wa´ka = the night will never come, it will never be night
i´ckik igla´hit = I burn by posteriors
icioho igla´xpati = I burn my mouth
la´xpa la´xpaxtc = daubed, spotted, large placards or spots on mats, animals, etc.
la´xpan u´kitani = I put in spots
la´xpi la´xpǎ = to cover, put on
la´xpani = I put something (on the table)
la´xspi la´spi la´spiku = (m.) metal, money, iron
la´spita eyo´koi = silver buttons worn on clothing
la´xspa te´pînto = see XV 119a, another variety of silver button
la´spi pa´tcu = minute silver pieces worn as ornaments on clothing
uxcuxsu ma´-isaxk lăxtca = blind in one eye
la´lak = woodcock, also given as bustard and wild goose (Creole outarde)
Gatschet gives hî´xku la´la tă which he interprets " big grunting mouse "; la´lak is also the word for " wild goose ", but it is uncertain whether they are the same)
a´ria so´lu la´maha ka´han pa´ha = if the doctor treating me does not cure there is no payment
ta lapauta = in order to land
u´ni lapa´ra = a responsible, honorable man
e´ti man´ la´pun = My friend, (are you) well?
lapwiya´mu = said to be a small variety of beaver
la´tcu = to hoe, dig with the mattock, cultivate
lă´wa = to stick out, pull back, move (said of the tongue only)
i´lu ho´lăwakani = I am pulling the tongue out
igle´-eki iglă´ki = I have lost in gambling
le´katic = west (f.), the sun loses itself
wica´ri lehi´ = thou hast indeed time
woru lehe ma´lexkini = I havee learned thoroly
lexki lixki = to pour, pour in
lexpi li´xpi = to enclose; by der. stopper and button
ti´xcuk le´xpi ta´saxtcuni = "to glue the door shut", hinges (?)
po´luxk le´xpi ta wa´xtcu = "to pull out the stopper", corkscrew
ikle´xtuti = something (such as a sore) burst on me
lēpi = a discourd, to discourse, to explain
Other examples all with ya´na v.I as in Haas's material
ti´ra le´yu = a straight strip of cloth
ha´li le´yun tuxk = strip of land
le´yuta a´ni = I go in a straight line
a´ha le´yuta = not at all alone
li´lixtcani = I wet something in places
a´xeuk ti´xkuan li´xtca = it is moist or foggy weather
li´ma = to finish burning, to consume
te´lia ta li´ma = "the soul burner"; i.e. the sorceress, the witch
lina = who resuscitates, always survives, indestructible (applies to persons)
sin´ric lo´ha = holes in their noses, in their beaks (f.)
lo´ki = (m.) a little hole full of water, water-hole
te´xkelu lo´xpu = a pear "a cone shaped apple"
ho-ukloxsaka = I draw him out of (something)
ni´ni ta´lo´xsa = a fish net, a seine
lo´la = name of an unidentified tree
ri´xku o´racki lo´pa = a withered or frozen leaf
ha´xcupa lo´ta e´rusa = I can run fast
ta´lu lo´ha = perforation in the ear lobe, etc.
lu´akani = I am stirring up something (flour, etc) with water or other liquid
ta loedja ho´xka = opening of the ear
a´luk tcoha = law, statute, "chief's tongue or words"
ya´na we´rusa yo´luk = thou knowest to speak words
yo´luk a-a´xkini = I keep the history, the relation
ta yo´luxtc = the language, words, a word
ala´kini = (said when a dog is lapping something in a pot)
wi´ci lu´mi = lukewarm water (G & V); warm (J)
lupuxkinaxkun = it is good that he is dead, like he was dead
o´ni lu´pi nu = cemetery, (or) someone is dead
sa´tǎ lupi´ wi´xti u´ra = a dead horse, swollen up, lying down (poison or in the sun)
iglu´pu-i = he is dead from me
lupăndja´ = I am going to die
-ma -man = suff. forming infinitives and participles, also independent personal pronouns; often used as the equivalent of the conj."and" , "and then"
o´ni ma´hon = a good for nothing person (indian)
o´roa o´ni maho´n ate´kpun = a mixed blood
u´ni ma´yuhuni = a person who has nothing to do
ma´-i = the one of the two, the other, the one , each
ma´-ita ma´-ixta = on the other (bank of the river)
ama-i ili = each one of them
cixkal maito´ro ukho´xkakan = I make a hole thru a stone
măka = to punish, give one bad luck,make him unlucky, do something he will not like, to hoodoo
umă´kati = it will not be good for him (v), something he does not like (j), punishment (g)
ma´kita = to hollow out (earth), they plow
ma´xka = to love, dear (an endearing term); to be dear, expensive
ma´xsata = six by six (g), the 6th one (v)
maxsi masi = to turn, to circle
yu´xki ta ma´xsini = "the turning nail", screw
ti´xcuma maxsikatan = I am roasting some meat
ma´maxsin = I turn myself many times
o´ni ri ta ma´xcu = a carpenter, a builder
ri´xku ri ta maxcu´ = wood to construct a house
maxtci´ = to gather, to pick (as cotton), to break a piece off (v) a piece
mali = to change, to learn by oneself, to change oneself into an animal (g), to accomplish or obtain supernatural power
worulehe´ ma´lexkini = I have learned by myself
exsini malikatani = I shake (i.e. change position of) the head
nu´xcti ya´wa maleki´ = sorceress, witch, "woman who does evil", a prostitute
ma´li tă ku´a ma´li tă = the scarlet tanajer; creole, anispatule
ma´li tu = the nonpareil or red-eyed vireo, creole ti gris
măra mera mira = cylindrical, around, something rolling (j)
ka´na măratan = what I put around (V & J), i circle or go around something (G)
yu´nka me´rku (or mi´rku) nălua u´witan = a spool of thread rolling of itself; Gatrch calls me´rku a "bobbin"
ta´rku (ta´xku) me´rkuni ku uknălaxkatani = I roll a wooden barrel
mata = to draw or bring water
melàkan = child! (in direct address), there was a chief of this name
melime´lita = many black persons or things
mihun mihuni = down there, far (more than a mile)
mi´hunixtc = down there (said of a village, etc.)
mix na´mu = How much (meat) there is!
ta ru´xtcu mi´ssin sa´ma = these plaquemines are ripe
ta ku´mac mi´xtatik = on the other side of the stone
ta´-uc ro´mana mi´ctîk ta ma´kak tikpe´ri = the water is heavier than the oil
mi´ctik he´kina = far (more than a mile)
to´ni mic uxki ku po´xkiti tcictaha = the man below is still visible
to´ni mi´ta pan u-a´ku = this man who passes there
mi´limi´lita = many red persons or things
mîra´ = to cast a reflection, brightness (of the sun only)
mi´rani = to cast a relection on someone, lightening (v)
mir ha´yic yu´kati = the sun shines
mi´ranuxki = he reflected, it has lightened (v)
tigmi´ruta = he lighted her with the reflection
mo´kukani ta mo´kuxta = I am sweeping with a broom
mo´lu = stuffed, filled up, filled with
ka´xcua mo´luni(i) = mattress filled with moss, a cushion (V.)
a´xcukun ta´xtcikc mo´lun = the setting sun (?)
e´yuxta mu´hăn = I carry in my arms
okatok e´yuxta mu´hi = to carry around a baby (J.)
e´xsin hamu´xkikan = I am bending my head
ru´xkic hamuxkikan = I am bending forward
i´matan hamu´xkă = I bend myself, bow
mû´xki mû´xke = smoke, to bucan, smoke-dry
a-i muxhe´ri tco´hatu = a steamboat captain, "little chief of a fire-smoke boat"
mu´xtul = swamp, snag, trunk; not swamp
ha´xku-mu´xtul = a cypress swamp, any swamp
muna = to flow, run (of liquids only)
wi´xci ta mona´ni = (m.) tap, spigot, faucet
ha´li mu´cali = a little mound, an elevation--sometimes as high as a house
na ni = seems to be a sign of the dual altho occurring in the plural of all obj. pronominal forms except one because with that exception the objective forms do not differentiate the dual and plural)
ta´na ni´xsa le´hi = a very new bed
naha = (m.) back (of anything), to lean against
ri´xku tce´xki ta´ nahan = back of chair
ha´lukin na´kêwatci = war, the war in a city, "d'un pays la guerre"
na´kši = is the French name [of the Natchez], says Sam
naxika = (m.) dogwood, arrow-wood; Creole, bois de flèche (somewhat uncertain)
lupuxhi naxkun´ = it's good that he's dead
lupuxhi naxkûn´ = he is as if dead
Some old-time name but I don't know it
na´xtcu = convex or protuberant (like breast of a woman)
ci´xkal tan nă´xtcu = some kind of rock sticking together, large stone having numerous round places on the surface
naxta = (m.) shore, bank, hillock, bluff, cliff, mountain
naxt ha´ria = high bluff or bank
na´xta a´mayi = (m.) each shore, the 2 shores
rixku nă´lani = wooden roller; beam, a rolling log (V.)
na´mi = to stink, smell badly; louse (m.)
namū = to remain with or live with
una´mukiwi = he had remained with him, you stayed with him (V.)
gives yǎ´xta a´naxkar(i) which he interprets " turkey jaw ", and also ei´la na´rka but both are prob.
na´rka tawatani = a bug that lives in rotten wood; about 1½ inches long
ta´ naruk tu´xsuri = a mortar stick, pestle
ri´xku tce´xki ta nuxti´ni = rocking chair
ini yu´ru tă´cpasa = my canine teeth
taku ni´kani = I have to say
ka´naxku´pan ta´-ak ni´ăhă = I have nothing to say
ni´xkir hi´pu = going to dance (J.); a dance accompanied by song as in older time (G.)
ni´xsa = new, young, just, at present, etc.
o´ka tuxk ni´xsa upi´ratu = a child that has just been born, or is just being born
ni´ma = a horned owl, 6-7 in. long with a big head & feather ears 3 in. long, it flies at night
ni´ri wăka = cemetery; a grave caved in (V.)
ta nictăn ta ni´cara = the stomach, & spleen (?)
extini´yu yi´ = qualmishness, "pain in my heart"
hixtc no´ni = I'm putting it there
o´ni lu´pi nu´ = cemetery, dead person laid there
ti´ra no´kän po´xtukani = I cut cloth to measure
no´kuc = black bear (also in Creek & Hitchiti)
ta no´ri = the anger, the borer
nuxka (nucrki) = to wipe off, rub, clean
ti´ra ta nu´xka = a wiping rag
ta´xkin ta nu´xka = towel, 4 skin wiper
nuxkikatani aloe´tca = I am rubbing my ears
on tca´-i (ili)si´nima = these 2 women (evidently connected); his wives
ta´-uki ta´ nu´xtin sowi´xkatan = I swing something (G.), a rocking-chair (V.)
watu´ruk ho´xsa nu´ma = "rotten wine", oleander (?), "crane creeper grape"
ǎ´cka a´nupal kalǎ´rani = I stand with legs joined
ō = excl. when one forgets something, also equivalent to English oh!
e´yu ogeste okecta = my left arm
u'djik o´xkali = paunch of a animal
o´xcta me´li = dark blue or green
o´xcta te´lin = (given by Gatschet as "green", but perhaps it was intended for o´xcta me´li(n))
o´maka = sorceress, one who poisons, witch; shaman
u´wi o´maka = sorcerer, lit. "who changes himself into an animal"; name of Baptiste Cassetête & his wife
o´ni o´maka = Indian shaman, "person who poisons" (?)
o´ntetic haic ma´ku = cream, on the milk, butter
o´tara ɔpatära = the caul, fat film enveloping the breast of the hog, leaf lard
tac o´wi = the sweat of human beings
sa´tă o´wi = sweat of a horse
o´yaho(ni) = a branch, limb; poss. also brush, brushes
rixk o´yahon rixk o´yahun ri´xhu o´yahun = the branch of a tree
pa´ha = no, none at all, not (sometimes pa + ha appear to be separated)
pa´hal = foot, sole (of foot), flat (of foot)
ăcka pahal = lower part of foot
ta pă´ka ri´ = a blacksmith's shop
un o´xcka pă´xka = man with flat feet
pa´xha = to be dressed up finely epsecially about the head
paxpaxka = to be dressed up finely epsecially about the head
kua lehe´ o´xsa tapaxka´ = a hat with a peacock's tail on it
upa´xkani = I plaster it on him (his head?)
ukpa´xkaxkiti = we put it on his head, or we put a plaster on him
yu´nka ri´xtcu ka´xci pa´xkal = double knot
ro´hin apa´xkal e´nixku hamo´xtu = triple paper
ha´li sa´man pa´xkal(i) = a brick kiln
paxsa = split, to split (along the grain of the wood) into thin boards, to rive, to cleave
la´spi pa´xtcu = minute silver pieces worn as ornaments on clothing
pala palu = to catch; win, gain
sa´ni pa´laxkini = I have won a bet
a´xcuk a la´bu pa´lan = happiness, also I had a lucky day (betting, etc.)
ni´ni ta pa´la = kingfisher (J. & V.), fish-line (S.)
pan = too, also, altho, each, every
Jo´niku yu´ru mictik Hănriku panu´ra = John is taller than Henry
